_meta.authoringNotes: Citations cross-verified against WHO Global Leprosy Strategy 2021-2030, Lockwood DNJ BMJ 2004 review, Scollard DM Clin Microbiol Rev 2006, and current WHO MDT guidelines.

h1Title: Leprosy: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Leprosy, also called Hansen disease, is a chronic infection of the skin and peripheral nerves caused by the slow-growing bacteria Mycobacterium leprae and Mycobacterium lepromatosis. It is curable with WHO-supplied multidrug therapy taken over 6 to 12 months, and is far less contagious than commonly believed — over 95% of people are naturally immune. India, Brazil, and Indonesia account for roughly 80% of the 180,000 new cases reported globally each year. Leprosy targets cooler areas of the body — skin, peripheral nerves, eyes, upper airway, and testes — and untreated it causes nerve damage, deformity, and disability. Early detection plus prompt multidrug therapy stops transmission within days and prevents almost all long-term complications.

keyStats.prevalence: ~180,000 new cases reported globally in 2023 (WHO Weekly Epidemiological Record)
keyStats.demographics: Men affected approximately twice as often as women in most regions
keyStats.avgAge: Onset typically age 5-50; peak in 15-35 year-olds in endemic regions
keyStats.globalCases: ~3-4 million people living with leprosy-related disability worldwide (WHO 2023)

definition: Leprosy (ICD-10: A30) is a chronic granulomatous infection of the skin, peripheral nerves, mucosal surfaces, and eyes caused by Mycobacterium leprae and the closely related M. lepromatosis. The organism is an acid-fast obligate intracellular pathogen that has the longest doubling time of any known bacterium (12-14 days) and a 5-7 year incubation period on average, ranging from 9 months to 20+ years. The disease unfolds along a spectrum determined by the host's cell-mediated immune response, classified by Ridley and Jopling into tuberculoid (TT), borderline tuberculoid (BT), mid-borderline (BB), borderline lepromatous (BL), and lepromatous (LL) forms, plus an indeterminate early form. For operational simplicity, WHO uses a two-class system: paucibacillary (1-5 skin lesions, no acid-fast bacilli on slit-skin smear) and multibacillary (6+ lesions, or any positive smear). Transmission is by prolonged close contact with an untreated multibacillary case, most likely through nasal mucosal shedding rather than skin contact. Over 95% of people are naturally immune. Care is led by dermatologists, infectious disease specialists, and dedicated national leprosy programs in endemic countries; ophthalmology, orthopedics, and physiotherapy contribute to managing established neuropathy and deformity.

typesClassification.description: Earliest stage. One or a few small hypopigmented macules with mild sensory loss; often heals spontaneously or progresses along the spectrum within 1-3 years. Slit-skin smear typically negative.

1-5 well-defined hypopigmented or erythematous patches with anesthesia and a thickened nerve nearby. Strong cell-mediated immunity contains the bacteria; smear negative. Treated with 6 months of paucibacillary MDT.
Unstable intermediate form with multiple lesions of varying morphology. Can shift rapidly toward tuberculoid or lepromatous ends with immune perturbation, often with reactions.

Numerous symmetric skin lesions or diffuse infiltration with leonine facies, eyebrow loss, nasal congestion, glove-and-stocking sensory loss, and high bacillary load on smear. Treated with 12 months of multibacillary MDT.
Peripheral nerve damage without visible skin lesions. Common in India. Diagnosis often delayed; requires fine-needle nerve sampling or PCR for confirmation.

Acute immunologic episodes that can occur before, during, or after MDT. Type 1 (reversal) reactions cause sudden inflammation of existing skin lesions and nerves. Type 2 (erythema nodosum leprosum) causes painful tender subcutaneous nodules, fever, and systemic illness — primarily in lepromatous disease.

primarySymptoms: Hypopigmented or reddish skin patches with sensory loss — the patient cannot feel light touch or pinprick over the lesion, a finding present in over 90% of paucibacillary cases.
Thickened peripheral nerves palpable as firm cords, most often the ulnar nerve at the elbow, the common peroneal nerve at the fibular head, and the greater auricular nerve in the neck.
Numbness and tingling in hands and feet, often in a glove-and-stocking distribution in advanced multibacillary disease.
Loss of eyebrows and eyelashes (madarosis) and diffuse infiltration of facial skin (leonine facies) in lepromatous disease.
Painless ulcers, burns, or injuries on hands and feet from undetected trauma due to sensory neuropathy.
Foot drop, claw hand, or wrist drop from motor nerve involvement — late signs of established nerve damage.
Nasal stuffiness, nosebleeds, and eventual collapse of the nasal bridge in lepromatous disease with mucosal involvement.
Painful red tender skin nodules with fever, joint pain, and malaise — erythema nodosum leprosum (Type 2 reaction).
Sudden swelling and tenderness of pre-existing skin lesions plus acute nerve pain — Type 1 reversal reaction, a neurologic emergency.
Reduced corneal sensation and lagophthalmos (inability to fully close the eye) from facial and trigeminal nerve involvement, risking corneal ulceration.
earlyWarningSigns: A new patch on the skin that is paler than surrounding skin and feels different to touch — typically reduced or absent sensation
Numbness in fingers, toes, or feet without obvious cause in someone living in or from an endemic region
A painless small burn, cut, or ulcer on the hand or foot the patient cannot remember sustaining
Family member or close household contact with diagnosed leprosy in the past 5-10 years
Persistent nasal stuffiness, frequent nosebleeds, or hoarseness in an adult from an endemic area
emergencySigns: Sudden severe pain along a peripheral nerve with new motor weakness — Type 1 reaction with acute neuritis requires urgent high-dose corticosteroids to prevent permanent nerve damage
Erythema nodosum leprosum with high fever, severe joint pain, and systemic illness — Type 2 reaction may require corticosteroids, thalidomide, or hospital admission
Acute eye redness, pain, or visual loss in a leprosy patient — possible iritis or corneal ulcer needing urgent ophthalmology
Severe infected ulcer or necrotic wound on an anesthetic foot — risk of osteomyelitis and amputation
Stevens-Johnson syndrome or severe hepatitis after starting dapsone — life-threatening and requires immediate medication review

causes.description: An acid-fast intracellular bacterium discovered by Gerhard Hansen in 1873. It has the longest doubling time of any known bacterium (12-14 days), cannot be cultured in artificial media, and grows preferentially at 30-33°C — explaining its predilection for the cooler skin, peripheral nerves, eyes, and testes.

A second species identified in 2008 in patients with diffuse lepromatous leprosy in Mexico and the Caribbean. Genetically distinct from M. leprae but produces an indistinguishable clinical syndrome. Responds to the same multidrug therapy.

causes.cause: Prolonged close contact with an untreated multibacillary case
causes.description: Household contacts of a multibacillary index case have 5-10 times higher risk than the general population. Transmission is most likely via nasal mucosal shedding of the bacterium during years before treatment. Patients become non-infectious within days of starting MDT.

Over 95% of exposed individuals never develop disease. Susceptibility is partly heritable: HLA-DR2 and HLA-DR3 variants predispose to tuberculoid forms; HLA-DQ1 to lepromatous forms. PARK2 polymorphisms also confer susceptibility, particularly in South Asian populations.

Nine-banded armadillos are a confirmed natural reservoir of M. leprae in the southern US. About 30% of new US cases occur in patients with no foreign travel and documented armadillo exposure, particularly in Louisiana, Texas, Florida, and Mississippi.

HIV co-infection, malnutrition, and overcrowding can accelerate progression to symptomatic disease. Diabetes also increases risk. Poverty is the strongest macro-level driver of incidence, primarily through density of contact and delayed diagnosis.

riskFactors.factor: Living in or originating from an endemic country
riskFactors.description: India, Brazil, and Indonesia together carry roughly 80% of global cases. Sub-Saharan Africa, Bangladesh, Nepal, Myanmar, and parts of the Philippines also have meaningful transmission.

riskFactors.factor: Household contact with an untreated multibacillary case
riskFactors.description: Household contacts have 5-10 times higher risk than the general population. WHO recommends single-dose rifampicin chemoprophylaxis for contacts after thorough screening.
Men have approximately twice the incidence in most endemic regions; reasons include both biological and exposure-related factors (occupation, migration patterns).
Most new cases present in this age range; rare under 2 years (the long incubation period accounts for this). Childhood cases indicate ongoing transmission.
Nine-banded armadillos are a natural reservoir in the southern US. About 30% of US cases have no foreign travel and traceable armadillo exposure (handling, hunting, eating).
Over 95% of exposed individuals are naturally immune. Susceptibility is partly heritable: HLA-DR2, HLA-DR3, HLA-DQ1, and PARK2 polymorphisms have replicated associations.
HIV alters immune response but does not strongly increase leprosy incidence; however, ART initiation can unmask previously subclinical leprosy as a paradoxical immune reconstitution event.

affectedDemographics: WHO documented 182,815 new cases globally in 2023; India alone reported over 100,000 new cases
India, Brazil, and Indonesia account for roughly 80% of new cases globally
US: ~150-200 new cases reported annually; ~30% are autochthonous (no foreign travel)
Children under 15 made up approximately 7% of new cases globally in 2023 — an indicator of ongoing transmission
Grade 2 disability (visible damage at diagnosis) at presentation occurs in roughly 6% of new cases globally — the operational marker of delayed diagnosis
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis is primarily clinical, anchored on the WHO three-sign rule: a definite diagnosis requires one or more of (1) a hypopigmented or reddish skin lesion with definite loss of sensation, (2) thickening or enlargement of one or more peripheral nerves with associated motor or sensory deficit, or (3) demonstration of acid-fast bacilli on slit-skin smear. Any of these signs alone is sufficient in an endemic context. Slit-skin smear is performed at 4-6 sites (earlobes, forehead, suspect lesions) by making a small superficial incision and scraping dermal tissue for Ziehl-Neelsen staining. Positive smears confirm multibacillary disease; negative smears do not exclude paucibacillary leprosy. Skin biopsy with histopathology is the reference standard, showing the characteristic perineural infiltrate, granulomas in tuberculoid forms, or foamy macrophages (Virchow cells) packed with acid-fast bacilli in lepromatous forms. PCR for M. leprae DNA has 80-95% sensitivity and is increasingly available in reference labs; it is particularly useful in pure neural leprosy where biopsy is challenging. Nerve function assessment — including monofilament testing of hands and feet, voluntary muscle testing of the small muscles of the hand and foot, and palpation of named peripheral nerves — is essential at diagnosis and serially through treatment. The Ridley-Jopling spectrum is determined by clinical features plus histology; for treatment, the WHO paucibacillary/multibacillary classification suffices. Distinguish leprosy from vitiligo (no sensory loss), tinea versicolor (positive KOH preparation), sarcoidosis (no nerve thickening), and post-inflammatory hypopigmentation (history of prior dermatitis).

diagnosticTests.purpose: Demonstrates acid-fast bacilli from dermal sites. Positive smear confirms multibacillary disease and quantifies bacillary index. Negative smear does not exclude paucibacillary leprosy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A clinician makes small superficial incisions at 4-6 sites (earlobes, forehead, suspect lesions) and scrapes dermal tissue onto a slide. Mild discomfort lasting seconds per site; no bleeding.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Reference-standard test. Identifies perineural infiltration (characteristic of leprosy), granulomas in tuberculoid forms, and foamy macrophages packed with bacilli in lepromatous forms. Classifies along the Ridley-Jopling spectrum.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Local anesthetic, then 4-5 mm punch biopsy of an active skin lesion. Mild discomfort; one or two sutures. Results in 5-10 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Sensitive and specific molecular confirmation, particularly valuable in pure neural leprosy without skin lesions and in paucibacillary cases with negative smears. Can also detect drug-resistance mutations.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Skin biopsy or nerve aspirate sent to a reference laboratory in transport medium. Results in 1-2 weeks.

diagnosticTests.test: Peripheral nerve examination with monofilament testing
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies sensory loss on hands and feet using Semmes-Weinstein monofilaments. Identifies subclinical nerve damage that should be monitored to prevent ulcers and disability.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A nylon filament is applied at standardized sites on the palm and sole; the patient indicates when they feel it. Painless and takes 10-15 minutes. Repeated every 3 months during treatment.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Grades strength of small muscles of the hand (abductor pollicis brevis, first dorsal interosseous) and foot (tibialis anterior, extensor hallucis longus). Detects early motor nerve damage that often precedes deformity.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Brief clinical assessment by physiotherapist or specialist nurse. Each muscle is graded 0-5 on the Medical Research Council scale. No equipment needed.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies lagophthalmos, reduced corneal sensation, iritis, and other ocular complications. Recommended at diagnosis and annually thereafter for multibacillary disease.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard slit-lamp examination, visual acuity, and corneal sensation testing. Performed by an ophthalmologist or trained clinical officer.

treatmentOverview: WHO-supplied multidrug therapy (MDT) is the global standard since 1981 and cures leprosy at every stage. MDT prevents drug resistance, halts transmission within days, and is provided free of charge by WHO to endemic countries through blister packs. Current 2018 WHO regimens are unified into a 3-drug combination for both paucibacillary and multibacillary disease, simplifying program logistics. Paucibacillary leprosy (1-5 skin lesions, smear-negative) is treated with rifampicin 600 mg monthly (supervised), clofazimine 300 mg monthly (supervised) and 50 mg daily (self-administered), plus dapsone 100 mg daily for 6 months. Multibacillary leprosy (6+ lesions or smear-positive) uses the same three drugs for 12 months. Doses are reduced in children and patients under 35 kg. Adherence is the strongest predictor of outcome; relapse after completed MDT occurs in under 1% over 10 years. Leprosy reactions (Type 1 reversal and Type 2 ENL) are major causes of nerve damage and require prompt treatment. Type 1 reactions are managed with prednisolone starting at 40-60 mg daily, tapered over 12-24 weeks based on nerve function. Type 2 ENL responds to prednisolone, thalidomide (in non-pregnant patients), or clofazimine dose escalation; severe recurrent ENL may require methotrexate or biologics. Single-dose rifampicin (SDR) postexposure prophylaxis is offered to contacts of newly diagnosed cases under the WHO PEP-LPEP program, reducing contact incidence by approximately 60%. Nerve function monitoring continues for at least 2 years post-MDT because delayed reactions and neuritis can occur. Disability prevention — daily inspection of insensate hands and feet, protective footwear, eye care, splinting, and reconstructive surgery for established deformity — is a parallel pillar of management. Drug resistance is rare but documented for rifampicin and dapsone; surveillance programs perform PCR-based resistance testing on relapses and treatment failures.

medicalTreatments.name: Rifampicin (600 mg monthly, supervised)
medicalTreatments.description: The most powerful drug in MDT. A single 600 mg dose kills 99.9% of viable M. leprae within 5 days, rendering the patient non-infectious almost immediately. Monthly supervised dosing prevents resistance and ensures adherence. Hepatotoxicity is the main side effect; monitor LFTs.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Single-dose bactericidal effect of 99.9%; integral to cure rates exceeding 98% in completed MDT courses.

medicalTreatments.name: Clofazimine (300 mg monthly supervised plus 50 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Lipophilic riminophenazine with both bactericidal and anti-inflammatory action. Reduces ENL frequency in multibacillary disease. Causes reddish-brown skin pigmentation that fades over 6-12 months after stopping treatment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Adjunctive cure contribution and 30-50% reduction in ENL incidence; pigmentation reversible.

medicalTreatments.name: Dapsone (100 mg daily self-administered)
medicalTreatments.description: Bacteriostatic sulfone, the original anti-leprosy drug from 1940s. Inexpensive and globally available. Risk of hemolytic anemia in G6PD-deficient patients (screen before starting); rare hypersensitivity syndrome (DRESS) within 4-8 weeks of initiation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Adjunctive cure contribution; lifelong monotherapy abandoned in 1980s due to resistance, but indispensable in MDT combinations.

medicalTreatments.name: Prednisolone for leprosy reactions (40-60 mg daily, taper over 12-24 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line for Type 1 reversal reactions with neuritis and severe Type 2 ENL. Suppresses immune-mediated nerve damage. Long tapers (12-24 weeks) reduce rebound. Bone, glycemic, infection, and ocular monitoring required.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Prevents progression of motor and sensory deficit in 70-80% of Type 1 reactions when started within 6 months of nerve damage onset.

medicalTreatments.name: Thalidomide (100-400 mg daily for severe ENL)
medicalTreatments.description: Highly effective for severe Type 2 ENL. Strict pregnancy-prevention program required due to severe teratogenicity. Sedation, peripheral neuropathy, and constipation are common side effects.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom relief in over 90% of severe ENL cases within 7-14 days; dramatic response often distinguishes ENL from other tender-nodule conditions.

medicalTreatments.name: Single-dose rifampicin (SDR-PEP) for contacts
medicalTreatments.description: WHO 2018-recommended chemoprophylaxis for asymptomatic contacts of newly diagnosed cases who screen negative for active disease and TB. A single 600 mg dose for adults; weight-adjusted for children.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces contact leprosy incidence by approximately 60% over 2 years (Moet 2008 COLEP trial).

surgicalOptions.name: Reconstructive surgery for established deformity
surgicalOptions.description: Tendon transfers (e.g., extensor indicis to abductor pollicis brevis for opponens loss), nerve decompression, claw-hand corrections, and foot-drop releases. Best results when performed after a year of stable MDT and rehabilitation.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Functional improvement in 70-85% of selected patients; cosmetic results vary with extent of damage.

surgicalOptions.name: Lagophthalmos correction (gold weight implant, tarsorrhaphy)
surgicalOptions.description: For patients with facial nerve damage and inability to close the eye, raising corneal ulcer risk. Gold or platinum weight implanted in the upper eyelid restores blink mechanics.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Adequate eye closure restored in 85-90% of patients; reduces corneal ulceration risk substantially.

whySeeSpecialist: All suspected leprosy should be evaluated by an infectious disease specialist, dermatologist, or designated leprosy clinic. In endemic countries, national leprosy programs provide free MDT and management. Pure neural leprosy, leprosy reactions, drug resistance, and ocular involvement require specialist input. Disability-prevention programs benefit from physiotherapist and ophthalmologist involvement throughout treatment and the first 2 years afterward.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek out a dermatologist or infectious disease physician with leprosy experience, ideally in or affiliated with a national leprosy control program. In the US, the National Hansen's Disease (Leprosy) Program (Baton Rouge, LA) is the federal referral center and provides free consultation and medications. WHO-endorsed centers exist in every endemic country. Continuity matters because MDT extends over months and nerve function must be monitored for at least 2 years post-treatment.
hospitalCriteria: Access to slit-skin smear and skin biopsy with experienced cytopathology
WHO MDT blister-pack supply and prescribing protocols
Trained physiotherapist or specialist nurse for nerve function assessment
Ophthalmology referral for ocular complication management
Linkage to national leprosy program for case reporting and contact tracing
keyFacilities: National Hansen's Disease (Leprosy) Program — Baton Rouge, Louisiana (US federal referral center)
Schieffelin Institute of Health-Research and Leprosy Centre, Karigiri (India)
Anandaban Leprosy Hospital, Kathmandu (Nepal) — INF-supported tertiary referral
TLM (The Leprosy Mission) global network across India, Bangladesh, Nigeria, and other endemic countries
Hospital for Tropical Diseases (London) for returning travelers and immigrant cases
preventionStrategies: Early case detection and prompt MDT — patients become non-infectious within days of starting rifampicin
Single-dose rifampicin chemoprophylaxis for household and close contacts under WHO PEP guidelines
Active contact tracing and screening every 6-12 months for at least 5 years after an index case
BCG vaccination provides partial protection (50-60%) against leprosy in endemic regions; routine in most endemic countries
Avoid handling, hunting, or consuming armadillos in US southern states where the natural reservoir exists
lifestyleModifications: Inspect hands and feet daily for cuts, blisters, and ulcers that may not be felt due to neuropathy
Wear well-fitting protective footwear at all times; avoid walking barefoot, especially on hot surfaces
Use heat-protective gloves and adequate cooking utensils to prevent burns to insensate hands
Protect eyes with sunglasses and lubricating drops if corneal sensation is reduced or lagophthalmos is present
Maintain regular physiotherapy contact during and after MDT for strength, joint range, and ulcer prevention
Inform employers, family, and clinicians about your diagnosis and complete the full MDT course without interruption

recommended: Balanced diet with adequate protein, iron, and B vitamins to support nerve healing and wound repair
Vitamin A-rich foods (leafy greens, mango, eggs) which support corneal health in patients with eye involvement
Supplemental folic acid during dapsone therapy to mitigate hemolytic anemia risk
avoid: Alcohol during rifampicin therapy due to additive hepatotoxicity
Fava beans and oxidizing drugs in G6PD-deficient patients on dapsone
Self-discontinuation of MDT — incomplete courses drive relapse and resistance

exerciseGuidelines: Regular exercise is encouraged with attention to protecting insensate limbs. Swimming, cycling, and walking in protective footwear are excellent. Avoid bare-foot activities, high-impact exercises that could cause unnoticed foot injury, and prolonged gripping that may damage insensate hands. Physiotherapist-guided range-of-motion exercises preserve joint function and prevent contractures.
dailyManagement: Take dapsone and clofazimine daily at the same time without interruption until the full MDT course is complete
Attend monthly clinic visits for supervised rifampicin and clofazimine doses
Inspect anesthetic hands and feet daily; clean and dress any wound promptly even if painless
Report any new skin lesion, nerve pain, sudden weakness, or fever during or after treatment
Continue nerve function monitoring every 3 months during MDT and every 6 months for at least 2 years afterward
prognosis: Prognosis is excellent with timely MDT. Cure rates exceed 98% in completed paucibacillary and multibacillary courses, and patients become non-infectious within days of starting rifampicin. Relapse occurs in under 1% of cases over 10 years post-treatment. Nerve damage is the major source of long-term disability; damage present at diagnosis often does not fully reverse, but early MDT and timely management of reactions prevent most new damage. WHO data show approximately 6% of patients globally present with Grade 2 (visible) disability at diagnosis — the operational marker of delayed detection. Leprosy reactions remain a challenge: Type 1 reversal reactions occur in 20-30% of multibacillary patients, often during the first year of MDT, and Type 2 ENL occurs in 20-50% of lepromatous patients, sometimes recurrently for years. Mortality directly from leprosy is rare in modern practice. Quality of life is heavily influenced by stigma, particularly in cultures with strong historical association; community-based rehabilitation programs and inclusion in mainstream healthcare have substantially reduced this over the past two decades. With sustained access to MDT and early diagnosis, WHO maintains the goal of zero new pediatric Grade 2 disability cases by 2030 as part of its global leprosy strategy.
recoveryTimeline: Skin lesions begin to fade within 4-8 weeks of starting MDT and continue to improve over 6-12 months. Nerve function in untreated cases often improves modestly with MDT alone, but established damage may persist. Patients are no longer infectious within days of the first rifampicin dose. Type 1 reactions resolve over 12-24 weeks with prednisolone tapers; Type 2 ENL may persist or recur for years and requires individualized long-term management. Clofazimine pigmentation fades 6-12 months after stopping treatment. Disability that remains 2 years after completing MDT is typically permanent and is addressed through rehabilitation and reconstructive surgery.
complications: Permanent peripheral nerve damage with sensory loss, motor weakness, and deformity — preventable with early MDT and prompt reaction management
Plantar ulcers, osteomyelitis, and progressive foot deformity from undetected trauma on anesthetic feet
Lagophthalmos, corneal ulceration, iritis, and blindness from facial and trigeminal nerve involvement
Type 1 reversal reactions with acute neuritis — a neurologic emergency requiring corticosteroids
Type 2 erythema nodosum leprosum with recurrent fever, joint pain, and systemic illness
Renal involvement (secondary amyloidosis) in long-standing untreated lepromatous disease
Stigma-related social isolation, unemployment, and depression — well-documented in endemic regions

supportResources.name: WHO Leprosy Programme
supportResources.description: Global program lead with country surveillance dashboards, training materials, and MDT supply coordination.

supportResources.name: National Hansen's Disease (Leprosy) Program (US)
supportResources.description: US Health Resources and Services Administration referral center providing free care, MDT, and consultation in Baton Rouge, Louisiana.

supportResources.name: The Leprosy Mission International
supportResources.description: Long-established global NGO providing clinical care, advocacy, and rehabilitation across endemic countries.

supportResources.name: ILEP (International Federation of Anti-Leprosy Associations)
supportResources.description: Federation of 13 anti-leprosy NGOs supporting WHO programs and rehabilitation worldwide.

confusedWithConditions.name: Tuberculosis (cutaneous)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cutaneous TB (lupus vulgaris, scrofuloderma) causes chronic skin lesions but does not produce sensory loss or peripheral nerve thickening — leprosy's defining features. TB lesions show acid-fast bacilli on biopsy and respond to anti-TB therapy, not leprosy MDT.

confusedWithConditions.name: Yaws
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Yaws produces raspberry-like papillomata on exposed limbs in children, with no sensory loss or nerve thickening. Yaws is endemic in similar tropical regions but is caused by Treponema pertenue and treated with azithromycin. Serology distinguishes them when biopsy is unavailable.

confusedWithConditions.name: Scabies
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Scabies causes intensely itchy papules in skin folds (wrist, finger webs, waistline). Leprosy lesions are not itchy and have reduced sensation. Scabies has visible burrows and S. scabiei mites on skin scraping.

relatedConditions.name: Tuberculosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Both caused by slow-growing mycobacteria with overlapping endemic geography; co-infection requires modified treatment regimens.

relatedConditions.name: Yaws
relatedConditions.relevance: Another tropical bacterial skin infection in WHO neglected-tropical-disease elimination programs.

relatedConditions.name: HIV/AIDS
relatedConditions.relevance: Common comorbidity in endemic regions; ART initiation can unmask subclinical leprosy through immune reconstitution.

relatedConditions.name: Anemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Dapsone in MDT can cause hemolytic anemia especially in G6PD deficiency; routine monitoring required.

faqs.question: Is leprosy curable?
faqs.answer: Yes. WHO multidrug therapy (MDT) cures leprosy completely — 6 months for paucibacillary and 12 months for multibacillary disease. Cure rates exceed 98% in completed courses, and patients become non-infectious within days of the first rifampicin dose. MDT is supplied free of charge by WHO globally.

faqs.question: Is leprosy contagious?
faqs.answer: Leprosy is far less contagious than commonly believed. Over 95% of people are naturally immune. Transmission requires prolonged close contact (usually household-level) with an untreated multibacillary case, most likely via nasal mucosal shedding.

faqs.question: How is leprosy spread?
faqs.answer: Leprosy spreads through prolonged close contact with an untreated multibacillary case, primarily via nasal droplets shed in the months to years before diagnosis. Household contacts have 5-10 times higher risk than the general population.

faqs.question: What does leprosy look like?
faqs.answer: Early leprosy typically presents as one or a few pale or reddish patches on the skin with reduced or absent sensation when tested with light touch. Advanced multibacillary leprosy may show numerous symmetric lesions, eyebrow loss, nasal congestion, and thickened palpable peripheral nerves.

faqs.question: How is leprosy diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is clinical, based on WHO criteria: a hypopigmented skin lesion with sensory loss, a thickened peripheral nerve with deficit, or acid-fast bacilli on slit-skin smear. Skin biopsy and PCR confirm the diagnosis. In endemic regions, trained leprosy workers can often diagnose at first visit.

faqs.question: What is multidrug therapy (MDT)?
faqs.answer: Multidrug therapy is the WHO standard regimen for leprosy: rifampicin once monthly, clofazimine daily plus a monthly larger dose, and dapsone daily. Paucibacillary leprosy is treated for 6 months; multibacillary leprosy for 12 months.

faqs.question: How long does leprosy treatment take?
faqs.answer: Paucibacillary leprosy (1-5 lesions, smear-negative) is treated for 6 months. Multibacillary leprosy (6 or more lesions or smear-positive) is treated for 12 months.

faqs.question: Does leprosy cause deformity?
faqs.answer: Untreated leprosy can damage peripheral nerves, leading to sensory loss, muscle weakness, and deformity (claw hand, foot drop, facial paralysis). With early MDT, most cases avoid permanent deformity.

faqs.question: Where is leprosy most common?
faqs.answer: WHO documented approximately 180,000 new cases globally in 2023. India, Brazil, and Indonesia together account for roughly 80% of new cases. Other notable endemic countries include Bangladesh, Nepal, Myanmar, the Philippines, Ethiopia, the Democratic Republic of the Congo, and Madagascar.

faqs.question: Can leprosy occur in the United States?
faqs.answer: Yes. About 150-200 new cases are reported annually in the US, roughly 30% in patients with no foreign travel — most often linked to armadillo exposure in Louisiana, Texas, Florida, and Mississippi. The National Hansen's Disease Program in Baton Rouge provides free treatment and consultation.

faqs.question: What are leprosy reactions?
faqs.answer: Leprosy reactions are sudden inflammatory episodes during or after MDT, caused by immune response to the bacteria. Type 1 (reversal) reactions inflame existing skin lesions and nerves, risking permanent damage; Type 2 (erythema nodosum leprosum) causes painful tender nodules with fever.

faqs.question: Can children get leprosy?
faqs.answer: Yes. Approximately 7% of new cases globally in 2023 were in children under 15 — an indicator of ongoing community transmission. Children typically present with paucibacillary disease. WHO weight-adjusts MDT doses for pediatric patients.

faqs.question: Is BCG vaccine effective against leprosy?
faqs.answer: BCG vaccination provides partial protection against leprosy — roughly 50-60% efficacy in pooled studies, though it varies by region. BCG is routinely given to infants in most endemic countries primarily for tuberculosis protection. Boosting BCG in adolescence may extend leprosy protection.

faqs.question: What is single-dose rifampicin prophylaxis?
faqs.answer: Single-dose rifampicin (SDR-PEP) is WHO-recommended chemoprophylaxis for household and close contacts of newly diagnosed cases. A single 600 mg dose (weight-adjusted in children) reduces contact leprosy incidence by approximately 60% over 2 years.

faqs.question: Why does leprosy affect cooler body parts?
faqs.answer: Mycobacterium leprae grows preferentially at 30-33°C, well below body core temperature. It therefore localizes in the cooler skin, peripheral nerves, eyes, upper airway, and testes. The face, hands, and feet — typically a few degrees cooler than the core — are the most commonly affected sites.

faqs.question: Can leprosy be passed from mother to baby?
faqs.answer: Vertical transmission is rare. Most children born to mothers with leprosy are healthy, especially when the mother is on MDT during pregnancy. MDT (rifampicin, clofazimine, dapsone) is considered safe in pregnancy.

faqs.question: Why does my skin turn red on clofazimine?
faqs.answer: Clofazimine, a component of MDT, accumulates in skin and causes reddish-brown pigmentation, particularly in lesional skin. This is harmless and reversible — the pigmentation fades over 6-12 months after completing treatment.

faqs.question: Will I always be at risk of relapse?
faqs.answer: Relapse after completed MDT is rare — under 1% over 10 years in WHO surveillance. Nerve function should be monitored every 6 months for at least 2 years after treatment because delayed reactions can occur. Any new skin lesion, sensory change, or nerve thickening should be reported promptly.

faqs.question: Is leprosy mentioned in religious texts the same as Hansen disease?
faqs.answer: Biblical 'leprosy' (Hebrew tzaraat, Greek lepra) referred to a range of skin conditions, most of which were not actual Hansen disease. Modern Hansen disease is a specific bacterial infection identified by Gerhard Hansen in 1873.

faqs.question: How much does leprosy treatment cost?
faqs.answer: MDT is provided free of charge by WHO worldwide through national leprosy programs. In the US, the National Hansen's Disease Program treats patients without cost regardless of insurance status.

metaDescription: Leprosy is a curable bacterial infection of skin and nerves. WHO multidrug therapy, symptoms, diagnosis, and disability prevention reviewed.

images.caption: Well-demarcated hypopigmented skin lesions of tuberculoid leprosy on the thigh.

images.caption: Late-stage leprosy with claw hand and digit changes from untreated peripheral nerve damage.
